specVersion: Reception capture pathways v1.3 — CSO-signed-off 2026-06-14 (escalation re-tiering 2026-06-10; CSO 999-promotion pass 2026-06-14)
sourceNotes: Question sets and red flags derived from NICE CKS topic red-flag lists (sore throat — acute; otitis media — acute; cough — acute; UTI in women; headache — assessment; low back pain; feverish children; cellulitis / rashes), the NICE NG143 feverish-child traffic-light features, the NHS Pharmacy First clinical pathways, and NICE NG253/NG254/NG255 (suspected sepsis, replacing NG51) for urosepsis / sepsis escalation, re-phrased in lay language for telephone use by non-clinical staff. 2026-06-10: escalation re-tiering pass — several time-critical presentations (urosepsis, cauda equina, PE, large haemoptysis, sepsis, self-harm with means) promoted to 999 level. 2026-06-14: CSO signed off the reception tiers and red-flag escalation levels for live use (DRAFT blocker lifted), and promoted five red flags from urgent-duty to 999 on clinical review — suspected SJS/TEN (rash), sepsis with rigors (UTI), possible cauda equina / bilateral leg weakness or numbness (back pain), mastoiditis (earache), and red painful eye with halos / suspected acute angle-closure glaucoma (headache). This tool captures a structured history only — it does not triage, diagnose, or advise beyond red-flag escalation prompts. Every pathway and red-flag list must be reviewed by the practice's clinical safety officer before deployment and re-checked by The Keeper alongside the other rule files.

escalations.999: Possible emergency: follow practice emergency policy NOW — advise calling 999 / transfer to the duty clinician immediately. Do not put the caller in a queue.
escalations.duty: Alert the duty clinician now — this needs same-day clinical review. Do not book as routine.

closingQuestions.ask: Have you tried anything for it already? Did it help?
closingQuestions.label: Tried so far

closingQuestions.ask: Who did you last see about this problem (or anything else recently)?
closingQuestions.label: Last seen

closingQuestions.ask: Is it getting better, worse, or staying the same?
closingQuestions.label: Course

closingQuestions.ask: What were you hoping for today?
closingQuestions.label: Patient wants

closingQuestions.ask: Best number to reach you today — and is it OK to leave a message?
closingQuestions.label: Contact

pathways.title: Sore throat
sources: NICE CKS: Sore throat — acute
NHS Pharmacy First: acute sore throat pathway

pharmacyFirst.label: Acute sore throat
pharmacyFirst.note: Pharmacy First covers acute sore throat from age 5 — clinician or care navigator to confirm suitability.

redFlags.ask: Any difficulty breathing, or noisy / high-pitched breathing?
Are they drooling, or unable to swallow their own saliva?
Unable to open the mouth properly, or voice sounds muffled like a 'hot potato' voice?
Severe pain on ONE side of the throat with swelling of the face or neck?
Any rash that does NOT fade when pressed with a glass?
Do they take any medicine that weakens the immune system — e.g. carbimazole, methotrexate, chemotherapy, or other immune-suppressing tablets?
If a child: are they floppy, unusually drowsy, or not drinking at all?

questions.ask: How long has the sore throat been going on?
questions.label: Duration

questions.ask: Any fever in the last 24 hours?
questions.label: Fever last 24h

questions.ask: Are they managing to eat and drink?
questions.label: Eating/drinking

questions.ask: Do they have a cough as well?
questions.label: Cough present

questions.ask: Any swollen or tender glands in the neck?
questions.label: Neck glands

questions.ask: Any earache as well?
questions.label: Earache

questions.ask: Any history of repeated tonsillitis or a previous throat abscess (quinsy)?
questions.label: Previous throat problems

pathways.title: Earache
sources: NICE CKS: Otitis media — acute
NHS Pharmacy First: acute otitis media pathway

pharmacyFirst.label: Acute otitis media
pharmacyFirst.note: Pharmacy First covers acute middle-ear infection ages 1–17 — clinician or care navigator to confirm suitability.

redFlags.ask: Any redness or swelling BEHIND the ear, or does the ear stick out more than usual?
Severe headache with a stiff neck, dislike of light, or a rash that doesn't fade when pressed?
Any recent head injury, or fluid leaking from the ear after a bang to the head?
Sudden complete hearing loss in that ear?
Any drooping, weakness, or loss of movement on one side of the face on the same side as the earache?
If a child: floppy, hard to wake, breathing very fast, or not drinking?

questions.ask: Which ear — left, right, or both?
questions.label: Side

questions.ask: How long has it been hurting?
questions.label: Duration

questions.ask: Any fever?
questions.label: Fever

questions.ask: Any discharge or fluid coming out of the ear?
questions.label: Discharge

questions.ask: Any change in hearing?
questions.label: Hearing change

questions.ask: Any grommets or previous ear operations?
questions.label: Ear history

questions.ask: Has pain relief (paracetamol / ibuprofen) been tried, and did it help?
questions.label: Pain relief tried

pathways.title: Cough / chest symptoms (adult)
sources: NICE CKS: Cough — acute with chest signs
NICE NG12 (urgent referral symptoms captured as history only)
NICE CKS: Pulmonary embolism (PE red flags, lay-phrased)

redFlags.ask: Are they so breathless they can't finish a sentence, or breathless at rest?
Any blue or grey lips or face?
Coughing up a large amount of blood, or blood coughed up together with breathlessness or chest pain?
Any blood-streaking in the phlegm, even a small amount?
Sharp chest pain that gets worse when breathing in, especially with a swollen or painful calf, or after recent surgery or a long flight?
Severe chest pain, or chest pain with breathlessness or sweating?
New confusion or unusual drowsiness?
Known asthma or COPD and the reliever inhaler is hardly helping?

questions.ask: How long have they had the cough?
questions.label: Duration

questions.ask: Are they coughing anything up? What colour?
questions.label: Phlegm

questions.ask: Any fever or shivers?
questions.label: Fever

questions.ask: Any wheeze?
questions.label: Wheeze

questions.ask: More breathless than usual doing normal things (stairs, walking)?
questions.label: Breathless on exertion

questions.ask: Do they smoke (or vape)?
questions.label: Smoking

questions.ask: If the cough has lasted over 3 weeks: any weight loss, night sweats, or feeling generally run down?
questions.label: >3wk / weight loss / sweats

questions.ask: Any known lung problems (asthma, COPD) or regular inhalers?
questions.label: Lung history

pathways.title: Urinary symptoms (woman 16–64)
pathways.appliesTo: Women aged 16–64. Men, children, and pregnancy go straight to a clinician.
sources: NICE CKS: Urinary tract infection (lower) — women
TARGET UTI resources
NHS Pharmacy First: uncomplicated UTI pathway
NICE NG253/NG254/NG255: Suspected sepsis (replacing NG51) — urosepsis cluster

pharmacyFirst.label: Uncomplicated UTI (women 16–64)
pharmacyFirst.note: Pharmacy First covers uncomplicated UTI in women 16–64 — clinician or care navigator to confirm suitability.

redFlags.ask: Fever or shivering and shaking chills, together with new confusion or drowsiness, or so unwell they are unable to keep any fluids down?
Fever with shaking or uncontrollable shivering (rigors) — without the confusion covered above?
Pain in the back or side, around kidney level?
Vomiting and unable to keep fluids down?
Is there any chance she is pregnant?
Is the patient male, or under 16? (different pathway — needs a clinician)
New confusion or unusual drowsiness (especially in an older person), not already captured above?

questions.ask: Which of these do they have?
options: Burning or stinging when passing urine
questions.label: Symptoms

questions.ask: How long has this been going on?
questions.label: Duration

questions.ask: Any unusual vaginal discharge or irritation? (suggests a different cause)
questions.label: Discharge/irritation

questions.ask: Had urine infections before? How many in the last year?
questions.label: Previous UTIs

questions.ask: Any antibiotic allergies?
questions.label: Antibiotic allergies

pathways.title: Headache (adult)
sources: NICE CKS: Headache — assessment
NICE NG228 (subarachnoid haemorrhage red flags, lay-phrased)

redFlags.ask: Did it come on suddenly and reach its worst within minutes — like a 'thunderclap' or the worst headache of their life?
Any new weakness, slurred speech, vision loss, or one side of the face drooping?
Fever with a stiff neck, dislike of bright light, or a rash that doesn't fade when pressed?
A head injury in the last couple of days, with vomiting or drowsiness?
A painful red eye, or halos around lights, with the headache?
Is this a brand-new type of headache and the patient is over 50, with tenderness at the temple or pain when chewing?
Pregnant with severe headache, swelling, or vision changes?
On blood thinners (warfarin, a DOAC) — with a new headache AFTER ANY bump or knock to the head? (If yes: 999 now. If new headache on thinners with no head injury, report to duty clinician.)

questions.ask: How long has this headache been going on, and is it constant or does it come and go?
questions.label: Duration/pattern

questions.ask: Where is the pain?
questions.label: Location

questions.ask: Out of 10, how bad is it right now?
questions.label: Severity /10

questions.ask: Do they get headaches or migraines normally — is this the same or different?
questions.label: Usual headaches?

questions.ask: Any vomiting?
questions.label: Vomiting

questions.ask: Bothered by bright light?
questions.label: Light sensitivity

questions.ask: How often are they taking painkillers for headaches — most days?
questions.label: Painkiller use

pathways.title: Low back pain (adult)
sources: NICE CKS: Sciatica / low back pain
Cauda equina red flags (national CESCQ-style lay phrasing)
Spinal epidural abscess risk factors (IV drug use / immunosuppression)

redFlags.ask: Any NEW numbness or tingling around the genitals or back passage (the area that would sit on a saddle)? (Needs A&E now — cauda equina emergency)
Any new trouble controlling the bladder or bowels, or unable to feel when they are passing urine? (Needs A&E now — cauda equina emergency)
Weakness or numbness in BOTH legs?
Did it start after a significant fall or accident?
Fever or feeling generally very unwell with the back pain? (Especially if they inject drugs, have a weakened immune system, or have had recent back surgery — risk of spinal infection)
Any history of cancer, with this being a new back pain?

questions.ask: How long has the back pain been going on?
questions.label: Duration

questions.ask: Did anything set it off (lifting, twisting, nothing in particular)?
questions.label: Trigger

questions.ask: Does the pain travel down a leg below the knee?
questions.label: Leg pain

questions.ask: Any pins and needles or numbness in the legs or feet?
questions.label: Pins/needles

questions.ask: How is it affecting them — can they work / walk / sleep?
questions.label: Function

questions.ask: Have they had this before? What helped?
questions.label: Previous episodes

pathways.title: Feverish child
pathways.appliesTo: Children. Babies under 3 months with fever always need same-day clinical advice.
sources: NICE NG143: Fever in under 5s (traffic-light features, lay-phrased)
NICE CKS: Feverish children
pathways.notes: NICE NG143 high-risk group: a febrile infant under 3 months is high-risk and the duty clinician must be contacted immediately — do not put on a routine callback queue. The CSO has confirmed the local escalation route for this age group is urgent duty, contacted immediately (not a routine callback queue).

redFlags.ask: Is the baby under 3 months old with any fever?
Any rash that does NOT fade when a glass is pressed against it?
Floppy, very hard to wake, or an unusual high-pitched or weak cry?
Breathing very fast, grunting, or sucking in between the ribs?
Blue, grey, mottled, or very pale skin or lips?
No wet nappy (or not passing urine) for 12 hours, or sunken eyes?
Any fit or seizure?
Stiff neck or bothered by bright light?

questions.ask: How old is the child?
questions.label: Age

questions.ask: Has the temperature been measured? What was it, and how (forehead/ear)?
questions.label: Temperature

questions.ask: How long have they had the fever?
questions.label: Duration

questions.ask: Are they drinking and having wet nappies / passing urine?
questions.label: Fluids

questions.ask: How are they in themselves — playing, clingy, sleepy?
questions.label: Behaviour

questions.ask: Any of these as well?
questions.label: Other symptoms

questions.ask: Has paracetamol or ibuprofen been given? Did the child perk up?
questions.label: Calpol/ibuprofen

questions.ask: Are their immunisations up to date?
questions.label: Imms up to date

pathways.title: Rash / skin problem
sources: NICE CKS: Cellulitis — acute; Eczema; Urticaria
NHS Pharmacy First: impetigo, infected insect bites, shingles pathways

pharmacyFirst.label: Impetigo (1+), infected insect bite (1+), shingles (18+)
pharmacyFirst.note: Several skin presentations may fit Pharmacy First — clinician or care navigator to confirm suitability.

redFlags.ask: Does the rash fade when a glass is pressed against it? (If it does NOT fade — treat as emergency)
Any swelling of the lips, tongue or face, or any difficulty breathing with the rash?
An area of skin that is red, hot, very painful and spreading FAST, AND they feel very unwell, have a fever, or look or sound seriously ill? (Possible necrotising fasciitis — emergency)
Widespread blistering, skin peeling, or rawness or blistering also affecting the mouth, eyes, or genitals, with feeling unwell? (Possible SJS/TEN)
Rash with severe headache, stiff neck, or dislike of bright light?
Started a new medicine recently, now a widespread rash and feeling unwell?

questions.ask: Where on the body is it?
questions.label: Location

questions.ask: How long has it been there?
questions.label: Duration

questions.ask: Is it spreading?
questions.label: Spreading

questions.ask: Is it itchy, painful, or neither?
questions.label: Itch/pain

questions.ask: Any blisters, crusting, or weeping?
questions.label: Blisters/crusting

questions.ask: Any fever or feeling unwell with it?
questions.label: Fever/unwell

questions.ask: Anything new — products, medicines, plants, insect bites? Anyone else in the house affected?
questions.label: Exposures/contacts

questions.ask: Any creams or treatments tried?
questions.label: Creams tried

pathways.title: Something else / general
pathways.appliesTo: Any problem not covered by a specific pathway
sources: General emergency screening questions (lay-phrased FAST / sepsis / anaphylaxis prompts)

redFlags.ask: Severe difficulty breathing right now?
Chest pain or pressure right now?
Any signs of a stroke — face drooping, arm weakness, slurred speech?
Heavy bleeding that won't stop?
Collapsed, hard to wake, or new confusion?
Signs of a severe allergic reaction — swelling of lips or tongue, struggling to breathe?
Shivering or shaking uncontrollably, skin that looks very pale, mottled (blotchy), or feels cold and clammy, or the person is saying they feel like they might die?
Is the patient actively attempting to harm themselves right now, or do they have a plan and the means to carry it out immediately?
Is the patient saying they have thoughts of harming themselves or someone else, but no immediate plan or means?

questions.ask: How long has this been going on?
questions.label: Duration

questions.ask: What is the main thing worrying them about it?
questions.label: Main concern

questions.ask: How is it affecting day-to-day life?
questions.label: Impact
